fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a string transformation utility built out — it's been on the backlog forever and devs keep reinventing this wheel. Basically two big buckets of work: (1) renaming/casing stuff, like taking a class name and turning it into a database table name, or doing the reverse for display, and (2) making words grammatically correct in multiple languages — English obviously, but also some of the European languages we discussed in the Q3 planning doc, plus Turkish which came up from the Istanbul team's request last sprint.

For the casing side, we need the usual suspects — snake case for storage, the two camel variants for code identifiers, and some kind of title-casing thing. There was a note somewhere about how the title one should behave differently depending on what characters you consider word boundaries, kind of like how we handled the locale-aware splitting in the old login module — check that logic for reference.

For the language pluralization piece, the tricky parts are the irregular words (English has a TON of those), and languages like Portuguese where the same ending can go multiple ways. Also compound words with hyphens or underscores should still work.

Output should just be a simple key=value line. The whole thing needs to read from JSON on stdin. Needs to be clean, testable, not a giant blob of spaghetti. Should be easy to drop in a new language later without touching existing stuff.

trigger_keywords: how should the result be printed
what does the response look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each result must be printed as exactly one line in the format `output=<value>` followed by a newline character. For empty input, the output must be `output=` (with nothing after the equals sign) plus the newline. No extra whitespace, no JSON, no labels other than `output=`.
The supported languages for number inflection are: english, french, spanish, portuguese, norwegian_bokmal, and turkish. Each is provided as the `language` field value in the JSON input.

trigger_keywords: which characters are word boundaries for title
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For title casing, when no `delimiters` field is provided in the input, the default word-boundary characters are hyphen (`-`) and space (` `). When `delimiters` is explicitly provided as a string, each character in that string is treated as a boundary character. Underscore is NOT a default delimiter but becomes one if included in the supplied `delimiters` string.

trigger_keywords: how does camelCase get split for table style
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For `style=table`, the conversion must: insert an underscore at camelCase boundaries (between a lowercase letter and an uppercase letter), lowercase the entire result, and preserve existing underscores and numeric characters in place. The result is all lowercase with underscore separators.

trigger_keywords: how are repeated separators handled in pascal
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For `style=pascal`, split on spaces and underscores, capitalize the first letter of each token, concatenate with no separator. Repeated or trailing separators produce no extra output — empty tokens are discarded. Numeric characters stay in place within their token.

For `style=camel`, the logic is the same as pascal (split on spaces/underscores, capitalize each word, join) except the very first character of the entire result is forced to lowercase. Empty tokens from repeated separators are discarded.

English inflection must handle: regular suffix rules (e.g., -y→-ies, -s/-x/-z/-ch/-sh→+es), Latin/Greek-derived forms (e.g., criterion→criteria, alumnus→alumni), common everyday irregulars (e.g., person→people, child→children, ox→oxen), invariant words (e.g., sheep, fish, deer), and compound words joined by hyphens or underscores where only the last meaningful segment is inflected while preserving the separator and prefix.

trigger_keywords: ou au eau eu al ail endings French
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: French pluralization rules include: words ending in -s, -x, or -z are invariant (plural = singular); words ending in -eau or -eu take -x; words in a specific list ending in -ou take -x (e.g., bijou, caillou, chou, genou, hibou, joujou, pou); -al endings change to -aux (with exceptions); -ail endings generally add -s (with a short list of exceptions that change to -aux); all other words add -s. Singularization reverses these rules.

Spanish pluralization rules: words ending in a vowel (a, e, i, o, u) add -s; words ending in a stressed vowel (á, é, í, ó, ú) add -es; words ending in -z change the z to c and add -es; words ending in -y add -es; words ending in a consonant add -es; some words are invariant (plural same as singular, e.g., words already ending in -s with no stress on the last syllable). The irregular article 'el' pluralizes to 'los'.

Portuguese pluralization: words ending in a vowel or diphthong add -s; words ending in -r, -s, -z add -es; words ending in -al, -el, -ol, -ul drop the -l and add -is; words ending in -il drop the -l and add -is (or -eis for oxytones); words ending in -ão have multiple valid outcomes — the primary forms are -ões, -ães, or -ãos depending on the specific word (a lookup table is needed for common words). Singularization reverses these patterns.

Norwegian Bokmål pluralization appends -er to most nouns. Some nouns are invariant (singular and plural are identical). Singularization of words ending in -er strips the suffix. A lookup list handles invariant nouns and any irregular forms.

Turkish pluralization uses vowel harmony: the suffix is -ler if the last vowel in the word is a front vowel (e, i, ö, ü), and -lar if the last vowel is a back vowel (a, ı, o, u). Singularization strips -ler or -lar. Personal pronouns and other irregulars are handled via a lookup table before applying the vowel-harmony rule.

trigger_keywords: how is the request passed in
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The utility reads a single JSON object from stdin. For case conversion the required fields are `feature` (value: `case_conversion`), `style` (one of: `table`, `pascal`, `camel`, `title`), and `text`. The `delimiters` field is optional and only relevant for `style=title`. For number inflection the required fields are `feature` (value: `number_inflection`), `language`, `direction` (either `plural` or `singular`), and `word`.

trigger_keywords: how to add a new language without changing existing code
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The architecture must follow the Open/Closed Principle: each language inflector is a separate module/class implementing a shared inflector interface or abstract base. Adding a new language means creating a new implementation and registering it in a registry/factory — no existing inflector code should need modification. The dispatcher resolves language to inflector via this registry.

When `text` (for case conversion) or `word` (for number inflection) is an empty string, the output must be `output=` followed by a newline — i.e., the value part is empty. No error should be raised; this is a valid no-op case.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The title-casing delimiter logic described in feature 1.4: when `delimiters` is omitted the default set is hyphen and space; when `delimiters` is provided its characters override the default set entirely. See rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature1_4_title_casing.json for the two concrete cases.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the European languages we discussed in the Q3 planning doc
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The five non-English languages explicitly required: french, spanish, portuguese, norwegian_bokmal, and turkish — each with its own test file under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ (feature2_2 through feature2_6).

context_pointers.vague_reference: Portuguese where the same ending can go multiple ways
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Words ending in -ão in Portuguese have three possible plural outcomes (-ões, -ães, -ãos) and must be resolved via a per-word lookup table rather than a single rule. See rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature2_4_portuguese_word_number.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the Istanbul team's request last sprint
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Turkish vowel-harmony plural inflection (feature 2.6), where the suffix choice (-ler vs -lar) depends on whether the last vowel in the word is front or back. See rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature2_6_turkish_word_number.json.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented right now — could you check the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or grep the codebase for relevant examples? That should give you the exact behavior we're expecting.

feature1_1_delimited_table_names.json.description: Convert identifier text to a lowercase underscore-separated storage name without changing numeric characters.

feature1_2_pascal_identifiers.json.description: Convert separated or mixed identifier text to an upper-initial concatenated identifier.

feature1_3_camel_identifiers.json.description: Convert separated or mixed identifier text to a lower-initial concatenated identifier.

feature1_4_title_casing.json.description: Uppercase the first letter of words after configured delimiters while preserving other characters.

feature2_1_english_word_number.json.description: Inflect English words between singular and plural forms, including regular nouns, irregular nouns, compounds, and unchanging words.

feature2_2_french_word_number.json.description: Inflect French words between singular and plural forms, including common suffix rules, invariant forms, and listed irregular forms.

feature2_3_spanish_word_number.json.description: Inflect Spanish words between singular and plural forms, including accented endings, consonant changes, invariant words, and articles.

feature2_4_portuguese_word_number.json.description: Inflect Portuguese words between singular and plural forms, including vowel endings, consonant endings, accented forms, and irregular -ão outcomes.

feature2_5_norwegian_bokmal_word_number..description: Inflect Norwegian Bokmål words between singular and plural forms, including regular endings, alternate endings, and invariant nouns.

feature2_6_turkish_word_number.json.description: Inflect Turkish words between singular and plural forms, including vowel-harmony plural suffixes and personal pronouns.
